questions.text: International Day of Yoga is celebrated on which date every year?
questions.explanation: International Day of Yoga is celebrated on June 21, the summer solstice (longest day in the Northern Hemisphere). It was proposed by PM Narendra Modi at the UN General Assembly in September 2014 and first observed globally on June 21, 2015.

questions.text: Kalaripayattu is an ancient martial art from which state?
questions.explanation: Kalaripayattu is the ancient martial art of Kerala, with a history going back at least 2,000 years. It is considered one of the oldest martial arts in the world. The word 'Kalari' refers to the training ground/school. It includes body exercises, stick fighting, and weapons training.

questions.text: Chess originated in India as 'Chaturanga'. What does Chaturanga mean?
questions.explanation: Chaturanga (Sanskrit for 'four divisions') was the 6th-century CE Indian predecessor to chess. The four divisions corresponded to the four arms of the Indian army: infantry (pawn), cavalry (knight), elephants (bishop), and chariots (rook). It then spread to Persia as Chatrang/Shatranj and eventually to Europe.

questions.text: National Sports Day in India is observed on which date?
questions.explanation: National Sports Day is observed on August 29, the birthday of Major Dhyan Chand — India's greatest hockey player, known as the 'Wizard of Hockey'. On this day, sports awards (Khel Ratna, Arjuna, Dronacharya, Dhyan Chand) are presented at Rashtrapati Bhavan.

questions.text: Thang-Ta is a martial art from Manipur. What do 'Thang' and 'Ta' mean?
questions.explanation: Thang-Ta (also called Huyen Langlon) is Manipur's ancient martial art. 'Thang' means sword and 'Ta' means spear in Meitei language — the two primary weapons of this art. It also exists as a dance form (Thang-Ta dance) performed at festivals. Sarit Sarak is the unarmed combat complement.

questions.text: Snakes and Ladders was invented in India as a moral teaching tool. It was originally called:
questions.explanation: Snakes and Ladders was originally known as 'Moksha Patam' (or Gyan Chaupar), created by saint Gyandev in the 13th century as a moral teaching tool. Virtues (like honesty, faith) were represented by ladders going up toward liberation; vices (like anger, greed) were represented by snakes going down to rebirth.

questions.text: Silambam is a traditional martial art from which Indian state?
questions.explanation: Silambam is the stick-fighting martial art from Tamil Nadu. It uses a bamboo staff and is referenced in ancient Sangam literature (Cilappatikaram, 2nd century CE). It was banned by the British in 1804 along with other martial arts but has been revived. It is distinct from Kerala's Kalaripayattu.

questions.text: Mallakhamb is the state sport of which Indian state?
questions.explanation: Mallakhamb (gymnastics on a wooden pole) is the state sport of Maharashtra. 'Malla' means wrestler/gymnast and 'Khamb' means pole. Performers execute yoga, wrestling, and gymnastic postures on a stationary vertical pole. It was traditionally used by wrestlers to develop strength, flexibility, and balance.

questions.text: Patanjali's Yoga Sutras describe how many limbs (angas) of yoga?
questions.explanation: Patanjali's Yoga Sutras describe Ashtanga Yoga — 8 limbs: Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama, Pratyahara, Dharana, Dhyana, Samadhi. Asana (postures) is only the 3rd limb; many people think yoga is only asanas, but the system is much broader, including ethical conduct and meditation.

questions.text: Gatka is a Sikh martial art associated with which community in Punjab?
questions.explanation: Gatka is primarily practiced by Nihang Sikhs — a traditional warrior order within Sikhism, identifiable by their blue attire and large turbans. Gatka uses stick (danda), kirpan (sword), and shield (dhal). It is performed at festivals like Baisakhi and Hola Mohalla (Anandpur Sahib).

questions.text: The Vallamkali (snake boat race) of Kerala is most famously associated with which festival?
questions.explanation: Vallamkali (snake boat race / chundan vallam race) is most famously associated with Onam, Kerala's harvest festival. The Nehru Trophy Boat Race on Punnamada Lake (Alappuzha/Alleppey) is the most famous. PM Nehru donated the trophy in 1952 after witnessing the race.

questions.text: Jallikattu is a traditional sport of Tamil Nadu. With which festival is it specifically associated?
questions.explanation: Jallikattu (bull-taming) is traditionally associated with Mattu Pongal — the 3rd day of the Pongal harvest festival in Tamil Nadu, which is dedicated to cattle. Bull-taming competitions are held in villages, especially in Alanganallur (Madurai district). The sport has faced Supreme Court and legislative scrutiny.

questions.text: Yoga was inscribed in UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in which year?
questions.explanation: Yoga was inscribed in UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2016. The International Day of Yoga had been established from 2015. The ICH inscription recognises Yoga as a complete system of mental, physical, and spiritual practice originating in India.

questions.text: The Dronacharya Award is presented to:
options: Athletes with consistent international performance over 4 years
Coaches who have produced outstanding sports persons
questions.explanation: The Dronacharya Award (established 1985) is given to coaches and trainers who have produced outstanding sports persons at the international level. Named after Drona, the legendary teacher of the Pandavas in the Mahabharata, it recognises the contribution of the 'guru' (coach) behind sporting excellence.

questions.text: Pachisi, the board game originating in India, is the ancestor of which modern game?
questions.explanation: Pachisi is the ancient Indian cross-and-circle board game (6th–7th century CE) that uses cowrie shells as dice. It was adapted by the British into 'Parcheesi' and eventually into 'Ludo'. The Mughal emperor Akbar famously played Pachisi on a large outdoor court using harem women as living pieces.

questions.text: India won all editions of the Kabaddi World Cup. In how many World Cup editions has India won?
options: India has won all editions (5 out of 5 as of 2023)
India has won 4 editions (lost one to Iran)
India has never participated in Kabaddi World Cup
questions.explanation: India has won all editions of the Kabaddi World Cup (2010, 2012, 2014, 2016, 2019). India is the most dominant nation in Kabaddi internationally. The sport originated in South India and is the national game of Bangladesh. The Pro Kabaddi League (since 2014) has greatly increased its popularity.
